output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.7, "R1.2": 0.95, "R1.3": 1.0, "R1.4": 0.6, "R1.5": 0.5, "R1.6": 0.5, "R1.7": 0.4}}

Summary: The section's headline claim (a ~35% reduction in magnitude-dependent bias) is not supported by any of the reported numbers; the evidence presented measures something else entirely. Several other gaps weaken the section.

Core problem — the central claim is unsupported
- The opening promises a debiasing method that reduces *magnitude-dependent bias* by ~35% and restores *near-uniform per-class performance across the apparent magnitude range*. None of the reported results measure either quantity. The metrics shown (accuracy, macro-F1, ROC-AUC over the *entire* test split) are aggregate, magnitude-agnostic scores. They cannot, even in principle, demonstrate that bias *as a function of magnitude* has been reduced.
- The aggregate deltas (+0.5 to +0.9 pp accuracy, +0.5 to +1.2 F1, +0.003 to +0.005 AUC) are also far smaller than 35%, so a reader cannot recover the headline number by any obvious transformation of the table. Either (a) the 35% figure comes from an unreported analysis that must be added, or (b) it is an overclaim. Without a magnitude-stratified evaluation — e.g., per-magnitude-bin Type Ia precision/recall, calibration curves vs. apparent magnitude, or a scalar bias metric such as the slope of P(Ia|mag) residuals — the central claim has no evidentiary basis in this section.
- Relatedly, the motivating claim that classifiers "over-predict Ia among bright sources and under-predict them among faint sources" is asserted but never quantified for the baseline, so there is no "before" against which the "after" can be compared.

Secondary issues
- "Across all three benchmarks … by approximately 35% on average" is the only place a bias number appears, and the dataset-by-dataset breakdown is missing. Averaging a relative bias reduction across three heterogeneous benchmarks can hide a case where the method actually fails on one of them; per-dataset values are needed.
- No uncertainty quantification. Differences of 0.5 pp accuracy on a single test split could easily be within statistical noise. Report bootstrap CIs, multiple seeds, or paired tests; otherwise the small aggregate gains read as cherry-picked.
- No baseline beyond "the same XGBoost backbone." For a paper claiming a survey-agnostic debiasing contribution, the natural comparisons are (i) probability calibration baselines (Platt, isotonic, temperature scaling), (ii) class-weight reweighting, and (iii) a magnitude-conditioned classifier trained end-to-end. Without these, the "delta vs. prior work" is undefined and a reviewer cannot tell whether RegMagD is doing something a one-line calibration call would not.
- "RegMagD" is named but not defined in this section. What does the regression layer regress, on what features, against what target? The reader needs at least one sentence specifying inputs (predicted class probabilities? magnitude alone? magnitude × probability?), output (recalibrated probabilities? a corrective shift?), and training signal.
- PLAsTiCC-2018 is largely simulated and uses redshift/distance modulus rather than survey-limit-dominated apparent-magnitude effects in the same way ZTF does. Treating all three benchmarks as interchangeable evidence for an "apparent-magnitude" bias story needs at least a sentence of justification.
- "Restoring near-uniform per-class performance" is a strong claim; soften or substantiate. "Near-uniform" needs an operational definition (e.g., max-minus-min per-bin F1 below some threshold).
- The cost paragraph ("under two minutes on a single CPU, <0.1 ms per alert") is good and should stay, but specify the CPU model and the alert feature dimensionality so the numbers are reproducible.
- Minor prose: "automatic evaluators in time-domain astronomy" in the final sentence is broader than what was demonstrated (three classification benchmarks with one backbone). Either narrow the conclusion or extend the experiments.

Recommended structural change
Insert a results subsection — before or in place of the current aggregate-metrics paragraph — that reports the magnitude-stratified analysis: per-magnitude-bin confusion statistics for Ia and non-Ia, the bias scalar that yields the 35% number, and a calibration plot. The aggregate table should be retained but demoted to a sanity check showing the debiasing does not cost overall accuracy.

Suggested ordering: (1) define the bias quantity and show the baseline's magnitude dependence; (2) describe RegMagD's inputs/outputs in one sentence (with a forward pointer to the methods section); (3) report magnitude-stratified post-RegMagD results with per-dataset numbers and CIs; (4) report aggregate metrics as a no-regression check; (5) cost.

Uncertainty disclosure
- I have not verified that ZTF-BTS, ALeRCE-DR1, and PLAsTiCC-2018 are named/configured exactly as stated, nor that the reported baseline numbers match published XGBoost results on these benchmarks; the citation/scholarship check is correspondingly partial.
- The 35% figure may be derivable from analyses elsewhere in the paper not shown here; my critique assumes this section must stand on its own evidence.
